intro.kicker: Topic 11 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Friction is a dissipative force; work is done against it whenever a body moves on a rough surface. On a horizontal surface: W = μmg × s (work against kinetic friction = Fk × displacement). On a rough inclined surface moving up with distance s: W = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ). NEET tests these formulas both standalone and as part of energy-dissipation problems. Understanding that work done against friction equals the mechanical energy converted to heat is central to energy chapter linkages.

expectedQuestions.description: Tested directly and as part of work–energy problems in NEET. Typical patterns: (i) compute work done against friction for a block moved distance s on a horizontal rough surface; (ii) compute work done by applied force on rough incline (W = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ) for uniform motion); (iii) energy dissipated by friction in a 2-body problem. Appears in nearly every other NEET paper.

timeRequired.description: 30 min on formula derivation and the θ=0 special case, 30 min MCQ practice. The horizontal case is directly obtained by setting θ = 0 in the inclined formula. Know that work done AGAINST friction is always positive (energy always leaves the mechanical system). Work done BY friction on a moving body is negative.

difficulty.description: Conceptually simple once the friction force is identified correctly. Main NEET trap: confusing work done BY friction (negative) with work done AGAINST friction (positive). Work done against friction = |Fk| × displacement = μmg cosθ × s for incline. On horizontal surface this reduces to μmgs. The formula W = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ) includes both work done against gravity (mgs sinθ) and against friction (μmgs cosθ).

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics addresses work done by friction as negative (work-energy theorem). NEET often asks for ‘work done against friction’ as a positive quantity, which is the magnitude of work done by friction. Students from US curricula should note this sign convention difference and practise both formulations: W_by_friction = −μmgs; W_against_friction = +μmgs.

weightage.sectionTitle: Work Done Against Friction — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears in 5 of 6 recent NEET papers — reliable scoring topic.
Horizontal surface W = μmgs is the most direct question type.
Inclined case W = mgs(sinθ+μ cosθ) tests both trigonometry and formula recall.

strategySteps.title: Learn Inclined Formula First
strategySteps.description: For uniform motion up rough incline: applied force = mg(sinθ + μ cosθ). Work done = force × distance = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ). Setting θ = 0 gives the horizontal case W = μmgs. Derive horizontally as a special case to avoid memorising two separate formulas.

strategySteps.title: Understand the Energy Split
strategySteps.description: W_total = W_against_gravity + W_against_friction = mgs sinθ + μmgs cosθ. Gravity component stores as PE; friction component converts to heat. NEET energy questions test this split explicitly.

strategySteps.title: Know the Three Dependences
strategySteps.description: Work done depends on: (i) weight of body (mg), (ii) nature and material of surfaces (μ), (iii) distance moved (s). It does NOT depend on velocity or time. This is a direct NEET conceptual MCQ.

cards.title: Work Done Against Friction Notes
cards.description: Derivations for horizontal and inclined surface cases, energy dissipation interpretation, work done by vs. against friction sign convention, and 8 solved NEET-pattern MCQs.

cards.title: Friction Work Energy Quick Reference
cards.description: Formula card with W = μmgs and W = mgs(sinθ+μ cosθ), worked examples for both cases, and the three variables that W depends on.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Work Done Against Friction

cards.title: Work Done on Rough Inclined Plane
cards.coreRevision: For uniform motion up a rough inclined plane of angle θ through distance s: Applied force F_app = mg(sinθ + μ cosθ). Work done by this force = F_app × s = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ). Of this: mgs sinθ goes against gravity (stored as PE), μmgs cosθ goes against friction (converted to heat).
points: W_total = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ) for uniform motion up rough incline
W_gravity = mgs sinθ (stored as gravitational PE)
Normal reaction R = mg cosθ; kinetic friction Fk = μmg cosθ
Work done against friction = Fk × s = μmg cosθ × s

cards.title: Work Done on Horizontal Rough Surface
cards.coreRevision: Setting θ = 0 in the inclined formula: W = μmgs. This equals kinetic friction force (Fk = μmg) × distance s. Work depends on: (i) weight mg, (ii) coefficient μ (material), (iii) distance s. It does NOT depend on velocity, time, or area of contact. This is a frequent NEET conceptual MCQ.
points: W = μmgs on horizontal surface (all work goes to heat)
Power dissipated by friction = Fk × v = μmgv
Heat generated = work done against friction = μmgs (always positive)
W is proportional to s (linear, not s²)
W does NOT depend on the velocity at which the block moves
cards.example: m = 10 kg, μ = 0.4, s = 8 m, g = 10 m/s². W = 0.4×10×10×8 = 320 J. All 320 J converts to heat. If the block was moving at 4 m/s, friction power = μmg×v = 40×4 = 160 W.

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics and US curricula compare to NEET scope on work done against friction.

gaps.title: Sign Convention: W_by vs. W_against Friction
gaps.description: AP Physics uses W_by_friction = −μmgs (negative, since friction opposes motion). NEET MCQs ask for ‘work done AGAINST friction’ = +μmgs (positive magnitude). Both conventions represent the same energy dissipation but answer choices differ. Read the question carefully to identify which convention is expected.

gaps.title: Three Dependences of W Tested in NEET
gaps.description: NEET explicitly tests that work done against friction depends on weight, μ, and distance — and NOT on velocity or cross-sectional area. AP Physics covers this indirectly; NEET makes it a direct assertion MCQ. 'If the block is moved at twice the velocity, work done against friction is —' must be answered: unchanged per unit distance (but power doubles).
bullets: W against friction = μmgs: independent of velocity.
Power against friction = μmgv: proportional to velocity.

questions.question: A block of mass 5 kg is dragged 10 m on a rough horizontal floor (μ = 0.3, g = 10 m/s²). The work done against friction is:
questions.explanation: W = μmgs = 0.3 × 5 × 10 × 10 = 150 J. This is the work done against kinetic friction, entirely converted to heat. Note: W does not depend on velocity; moving at 1 m/s or 10 m/s over 10 m gives the same W = 150 J.

questions.question: Work done against friction when a 2 kg block is moved up a 30° rough incline (μ = 0.2) through 5 m at uniform velocity (g = 10 m/s²) is:
questions.explanation: Work done against friction (only the friction component) = μmg cosθ × s = 0.2×2×10×cos30°×5 = 0.2×20×0.866×5 = 17.3 J. Total work by applied force = mgs(sinθ+μ cosθ) = 20×5×(0.5+0.173) = 67.3 J; of which 50 J goes against gravity and 17.3 J against friction.

questions.question: A 3 kg block is moved horizontally 6 m on a surface with μ = 0.5 (g = 10 m/s²). Work done against friction:
Work done against friction does NOT depend on which of the following?
A block is moved up a rough incline. Total work done by applied force = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ). The portion dissipated as heat is:
If a block is pushed 4 m on a floor with friction, then pulled back 4 m on the same floor, total work done against friction is:

items.title: Write both work-against-friction formulas
items.description: Horizontal: W = μmgs. Inclined (uniform motion up): W_friction = μmgs cosθ. Total applied work = mgs(sinθ+μ cosθ). Know which is total and which is friction-only portion.

items.title: State what W depends on
items.description: W = μmgs depends on (i) mass, (ii) μ (nature of surfaces), (iii) distance s. NOT on velocity, time, or area of contact. One-liner for conceptual MCQ.

items.title: Link to energy dissipation
items.description: Work done against friction = heat generated = μmgs (horizontal) or μmgs cosθ (incline). This is the mechanical energy lost to friction. Always positive.

items.title: Handle round-trip problems
items.description: For a body moved out and back (same path, same distance each way), total work against friction = 2μmgs (friction acts both ways). Net displacement = 0 but total work against friction is not zero.

items.question: Is work done against friction the same as heat generated?
items.answer: Yes. By the work-energy theorem, when a block moves at constant velocity on a rough surface, all the work done by the applied force goes against friction and is converted to heat (and sound). Numerically: heat generated = work done against friction = Fk × displacement = μmgs (horizontal). This equivalence is the basis of many NEET energy-friction numerical problems.

items.question: Why is work done against friction path-dependent but not velocity-dependent?
items.answer: Friction force magnitude = μmg (horizontal) is constant, so W = μmg × path length. Longer path = more work against friction. Velocity does not appear in the work formula (W = F × s, not F × v). However, POWER against friction = F × v = μmg × v is velocity-dependent.

items.question: What is the difference between work done by friction and work done against friction?
items.answer: Work done BY friction on a sliding block is negative: W_by = −μmgs (friction opposes motion, so force and displacement are anti-parallel). Work done AGAINST friction is positive: W_against = +μmgs (it is the work you must do to overcome friction). In energy accounting: W_against = heat generated. NEET questions use 'against friction' to ask for positive energy dissipation.

items.question: For a block pushed 5 m forward then pulled 5 m back, is the net work against friction zero?
items.answer: No. Friction always opposes motion, so work is done against it in both directions. Going forward: W1 = μmg×5. Coming back: W2 = μmg×5. Total = 2μmg×5 = 10μmg J. Net mechanical displacement is zero but total energy dissipated by friction is 10μmg J.

grounding_summary: Page 240 (textbook p. 227) derives work done for motion up a rough inclined surface: W = mgs(sinθ + μ cosθ) using W = force × distance. The horizontal case W = μmgs is obtained by setting θ = 0.
The textbook lists three explicit dependences: weight of the body, material and nature of surface in contact, and distance moved — confirming NEET conceptual MCQ content.
Page 241 reinforces the horizontal case: W = μmgs, with the same dependence list. Both pages confirm the inclined formula as the general case.
grounding_excerpt: moved up slowly on a rough inclined plane through distance
It is clear that work done depends upon

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction and Work)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for work done against friction formulas for horizontal and inclined planes, with explicit derivation in the Friction chapter.

sources.title: NEET Physics Class 11 Chapter 6 Friction Notes — ClassNotes.guru
sources.why_used: Verified NEET-targeted notes confirming work-done-against-friction as a key NEET exam topic and the three dependence variables tested in MCQs.

review.notes: Content grounded on page-240 OCR. W = mu*mgs (horizontal) and W = mgs(sin_theta + mu*cos_theta) (incline) derived from textbook. Three dependences listed verbatim from textbook.
